summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that a single crafted HTTP POST request to any oRPC procedure endpoint pollutes Object.prototype on the server process via the __proto__ path traversal in the deserialization metadata. The exploit uses meta type 2 (NaN) to assign a distinguishable value (NaN) to Object.prototype.polluted.
expected_security_impact: After sending a POST request with body {"json":{},"meta":[[2,"__proto__","polluted"]]}}, the server's Object.prototype.polluted property should be set to NaN. A subsequent request to the same procedure should detect the polluted property on a freshly created empty object, demonstrating global prototype pollution that persists across all requests.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh installed dependencies, started the oRPC server via tsx, sent the crafted payload to the /ping procedure endpoint, and verified prototype pollution. The first normal request returned {"json":"pong"}. The crafted payload request returned {"json":"POLLUTED: polluted=NaN"}, confirming the server handler detected the polluted property. A follow-up verification request also returned {"json":"POLLUTED: polluted=NaN"}, confirming the pollution persists on Object.prototype.
verification_method: The server handler creates a fresh empty object literal ({}) and checks whether ({}).polluted is NaN using Number.isNaN(). Since the empty object has no own property named 'polluted', the value must come from the prototype chain. The handler returns 'POLLUTED: polluted=NaN' if the pollution succeeded, or 'pong' otherwise. The exploit.mjs script sends the crafted payload, then sends a verification request and checks the response for the string 'POLLUTED:'. This distinguishes genuine prototype pollution from a server startup failure, request parsing error, or unrelated crash.
evidence_produced_by_run: The first POST request with a normal body returned {"json":"pong"}, confirming the server and procedure work correctly before the exploit.

The crafted POST request with body {"json":{},"meta":[[2,"__proto__","polluted"]]} returned {"json":"POLLUTED: polluted=NaN"}, confirming the deserializer processed the malicious meta array and the handler detected the polluted prototype.
The follow-up verification request also returned {"json":"POLLUTED: polluted=NaN"}, confirming that Object.prototype.polluted = NaN persists globally across requests.
The server remained operational after the pollution, demonstrating the attack does not cause an immediate crash and the polluted state persists for all subsequent operations.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
